2026-06-17T15:43:32.7999985Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8000460Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8000992Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8001533Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8002083Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8002523Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8002976Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8003614Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8004180Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8004607Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8004976Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8005393Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8005769Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8006252Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8006779Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8007270Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8007724Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8008191Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8008576Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8008793Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8009119Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8009519Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8010302Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8010817Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:32.8011337Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-17T15:43:53.2303740Z [36;1m# Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:53.2304598Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:53.2305339Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:53.2305938Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-17T15:43:53.2306410Z [36;1m[0m

No dist/ files appear anywhere in the 44-file diff. extension.mjs resolves DIST_DIR = join(__extensionDir, 'dist') and passes it to createHandler, which calls readFileSync for every static asset is a correctness regression that misleads every installer.",
2026-06-17T15:52:58.6659812Z       "file": "packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/README.md",
2026-06-17T15:53:01.1544417Z       "suggestion": "Remove the 'ships both a pre-built dist/' claim and the Package layout line 'dist/ -- pre-built client --. Without dist/, every canvas page load returns 404 and the extension is unusable. The PR body claims 'works immediately on apm install' -- that is false DO NOT EDIT directly'. Under Install (end users), add a build prerequisite step: 'cd packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/extensions/issue-monitor/client && npm install && npm run build && in current state. The README contradicts itself: it says 'always commit dist/ alongside source changes' but the gitignore prevents this without git add - cd ../../../../../../..'. Alternatively, commit dist/ and remove it from .gitignore, but that is a separate decision for the author."
2026-06-17T15:53:01.1548409Z     },

2026-06-17T15:53:04.2548760Z       "rationale": "The README dev workflow shows 'node --test tests/logic.test.mjs tests/server.test.mjs'. The PR adds five test files: api-service.test.mjs, logic.test.mjs, markdown.test.mjs, server.test.mjs, stores.test.mjs. The stores suite runs 'cd .apm/extensions/issue-monitor/client && npm ci && npm run build' so apm install materialises dist/ on the user's machine automatically. Pick one pattern and remove the other from the requires 'node --loader ./tests/solid-loader.mjs --test tests/stores.test.mjs' because it imports solid-js. A contributor following the README will run an incomplete test suite and may ship regressions in the stores or README."

2026-06-17T15:56:21.5954541Z {
2026-06-17T15:56:22.6714912Z   "persona": "auth-expert",
2026-06-17T15:56:22.6715505Z   "active": true,
2026-06-17T15:56:22.6737475Z   "summary": "CSRF+execFile model is sound; four recommended gaps: query-string CSRF bypass (inert but fragile), absent-origin passthrough, stale permission cache, /approve-workflow-runs accepts any branch.",
2026-06-17T15:56:22.6739037Z   "findings": [
2026-06-17T15:56:22.6739410Z     {
2026-06-17T15:56:22.6746260Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-17T15:56:23.7892037Z       "summary": "CSRF guard uses req.url verbatim; POST /merge-when-ready?x=1 bypasses WRITE_ENDPOINTS check",
2026-06-17T15:56:26.1598786Z       "rationale": "WRITE_ENDPOINTS.has(req.url) fails when a query string is appended, so the CSRF check is skipped. But this is a fragile two-lock coincidence -- any future handler that uses startsWith or a regex match on the path will silently inherit a CSRF-bypass for query-param requests. 2026-06-17T15:59:19.4316741Z {"persona": "python-architect", "active": true, "summary": "New JS canvas pkg: DI handler and pure logic module are solid; hardcoded REPO, module-global state, absent dist/, and prompt injection in /refine-comment need addressing.", "findings": [{"severity": "recommended", "summary": "REPO = microsoft/apm hardcoded; any installer of this package silently receives microsoft/apm data instead of their own repo", "rationale": "extension.mjs line 14 hardcodes REPO = microsoft/apm. The apm.yml description says Live contributor dashboard canvas for GitHub repositories (implying any repo) and the PR body says for any GitHub repository. A developer who installs this package for their own project gets microsoft/apm issues and PRs -- a silent correctness failure. The fix is trivial: read REPO from a config key injected at install time.", "file": "packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/extensions/issue-monitor/extension.mjs", "line": 14, "suggestion": "Replace const REPO = microsoft/apm with const REPO = process.env.APM_DASHBOARD_REPO || microsoft/apm. Expose a config.repo key in the package apm.yml that the apm CLI injects as APM_DASHBOARD_REPO at install time."}, {"severity": "recommended", "summary": "7 module-level let vars in extension.mjs are the Node.js equivalent of Python module-global mutable state -- untestable and concurrency-unsafe", "rationale": "issueData, prData, lastUpdated, lastError, pollTimer, openInstanceCount, and lastPrFingerprint are bare module-globals. server-handler.mjs correctly uses DI via createHandler(deps) with getIssueData/getPrData getter closures, but those getters close over module globals. This is structurally identical to the Python antipattern APM avoids: state cannot be reset between test instances, breaks if two canvas instances share a process, and makes the open/onClose lifecycle opaque. APM DiagnosticCollector collect-then-render pattern applies: encapsulate state in a DashboardStore class and pass the instance through DI.", "file": "packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/extensions/issue-monitor/extension.mjs", "line": 20, "suggestion": "Create class DashboardStore with issueData, prData, lastUpdated, lastError, pollTimer, openInstanceCount as instance fields and explicit getters/setters. Instantiate once at top-level and pass as deps.store to createHandler. Canvas action handlers call store.setIssueData() instead of mutating module globals."}, {"severity": "recommended", "summary": "dist/ referenced in code and PR description but absent from the 44 committed files; package will not serve any UI on apm install", "rationale": "extension.mjs constructs DIST_DIR = join(__extensionDir, dist) and server-handler.mjs serves all static assets from it. However, no dist/ files appear in the 44 committed files and the GitHub API directory listing for the extension folder shows no dist/ entry. If dist/ is gitignored, every page request returns 404 after install. A local process holding a valid CSRF token can inject arbitrary Copilot instructions via a crafted title containing backtick or heading sequences. Localhost+CSRF reduces the attack surface but does not eliminate it; prompt injection through user-controlled fields is a well-known LLM attack class.", "file": "packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/extensions/issue-monitor/server-handler.mjs", "suggestion": "Add a sanitize(str) helper that strips backtick, angle bracket, and leading # characters from title before interpolation. x-canvas-token==csrfToken?\"}\n    W -- no --> X[\"403 Forbidden\"]\n    W -- yes --> Y[\"[EXEC] ghExec: gh pr review --approve\"]\n    Y --> Z[\"JSON ok:true response to browser\"]\n    P2[\"setInterval fires every 30s\"] --> G", "sequence": "sequenceDiagram\n    participant U as User\n    participant APM as apm CLI\n    participant EXT as extension.mjs\n    participant GH as gh CLI\n    participant SDK as Copilot SDK\n    participant BR as Browser Canvas Panel\n    U->>APM: apm install apm-contributor-dashboard\n    APM->>EXT: load extension via canvas runtime\n    EXT->>SDK: joinSession + createCanvas\n    SDK-->>EXT: session handle\n    U->>BR: open APM Contributor Dashboard\n    BR->>SDK: canvas open event\n    SDK->>EXT: Canvas.open handler\n    EXT->>GH: gh issue list --repo REPO\n    GH-->>EXT: issue JSON array\n    EXT->>GH: gh pr list + gh run list per branch\n    GH-->>EXT: PR and workflow run JSON\n    EXT->>BR: return title + HTTP server URL\n    BR->>EXT: GET /index.html\n    EXT-->>BR: HTML with injected csrfToken\n    BR->>EXT: GET /api/issues\n    EXT-->>BR: JSON from module-global issueData\n    U->>BR: click Approve PR button\n    BR->>EXT: POST /approve-pr x-canvas-token header\n    EXT->>GH: gh pr review --approve\n    GH-->>EXT: success\n    EXT-->>BR: JSON ok true"}}}● Python-architect Review PR #1825 as Python Architect

2026-06-17T16:03:10.0562060Z ```json
2026-06-17T16:03:10.0562365Z {
2026-06-17T16:03:11.1489941Z   "headline": "Adds APM's first first-party canvas package -- a Copilot contributor dashboard with 186 JS tests and a sound security core -- but ships non-functional on apm install because dist/ is gitignored; rework needed on three fronts.",
2026-06-17T16:03:26.4335639Z   "arbitration": "This PR ships APM's most significant first-party proof-point to date: a Copilot-native contributor dashboard that demonstrates the canvas extension model in production, using APM to manage APM's own open-source contributor workflow. The 186-test suite, CSRF guard, execFile boundary, and path-traversal validation represent a genuinely strong security and quality baseline. Two panelists (devx-ux-expert, doc-writer) independently reached the same hard stop: dist/ is gitignored and not committed, meaning every user who follows the README install instructions receives a canvas extension that mounts with no client assets and returns 404 on every page request. The README simultaneously claims pre-built assets ship with the package. This is a correctness regression for every installer on the current commit.\n\nPanel convergence is high on three required fixes. First, the dist/ absence is the highest-priority mechanical issue: either commit a built dist/ at merge time or add a build step to the extension activation hook, and update the README to accurately describe the build requirement. Second, three panelists (supply-chain-security-expert, python-architect, auth-expert) independently flagged that user-controlled GitHub issue/PR title and user draft are interpolated raw into session.send() prompts in /refine-comment; the auth-expert classified this as self-injection-only (nit) but the supply-chain framing is stronger -- GitHub issue titles are adversary-controlled inputs in any multi-contributor repo and must be treated as untrusted before they reach a Copilot session. Third, apm.yml and the README both claim the package works for any GitHub repository while REPO is hardcoded to microsoft/apm; three panelists independently flag this as a broken user promise that must be corrected before the package is published.\n\nOnce these three root issues are resolved, this is a strong ship. The dogfooding loop -- APM managing APM's dashboard via APM -- is the most concrete proof-of-concept the project has shipped, and the 186 tests give the community a credibility anchor. The remaining recommended findings (CSRF query-string fragility, permission cache TTL, prerequisite guard in README) are real but bounded; they should be tracked as follow-up issues rather than held against this PR.",
2026-06-17T16:03:28.8803542Z   "dissent_notes": "auth-expert classified the title/draft prompt injection finding as a nit ('self-injection only'), while supply-chain-security-expert and python-architect both classified it as recommended. The self-injection framing is technically accurate for the current single-tenant scope, but GitHub issue and PR titles are adversary-controlled inputs in any multi-contributor environment. The supply-chain framing is correct and takes priority here: treat this as recommended, not a nit. No other severity disagreements were observed across active panelists.",

2026-06-17T16:03:30.0465293Z     "portable_by_manifest": "REPO = 'microsoft/apm' is hardcoded at the module level; any installer silently receives microsoft/apm data regardless of their own manifest, directly violating the portable-by-manifest promise.",
2026-06-17T16:03:31.2789094Z     "secure_by_default": "CSRF, execFile, and path-traversal guards are present and sound; prompt injection via GitHub-controlled title/draft into session.send() is the one gap that must be closed before this surface holds secure-by-default.",

2026-06-17T16:03:51.1350237Z       "why": "POST /merge-when-ready?x=1 currently bypasses the WRITE_ENDPOINTS check because req.url includes the query string. The coincidence that no write endpoint path today matches a path-plus-query-string is a fragile two-lock coincidence, not a durable defense; one endpoint rename would silently re-open the bypass."
2026-06-17T16:03:51.1354385Z     },

The prompt injection gap and REPO scope mismatch are security and user-promise issues that should not ship in a first-party package. All three have clear, bounded fixes. The underlying contribution -- first-party canvas extension, solid security core, 186 tests, dogfooding loop -- is genuinely strong and worth shipping quickly once the three root issues are resolved. Recommend the author: (1) resolve the dist/ commit strategy and update README to match, (2) sanitize title/draft before session.send() in /refine-comment, and (3) align apm.yml description to single-repo scope or make REPO configurable via manifest. 2026-06-17T16:12:19.6978233Z **Summary:** `needs_rework` — two blocking-severity findings from devx-ux-expert and doc-writer converge on the same root cause: `dist/` is gitignored and not committed, so `apm install` delivers a non-functional canvas extension (every page request returns 404). The README simultaneously claims pre-built assets ship. Three additional high-signal recommended fixes: prompt injection via GitHub-controlled issue titles into `session.send()`, hardcoded `REPO = "microsoft/apm"` vs. the apm.yml generic-scope claim, and a fragile CSRF query-string bypass. The underlying contribution (186 JS tests, sound security core, strong dogfooding story) is worth shipping quickly once those three root issues are addressed.

2026-06-17T16:12:23.7846283Z [191] ◐ Now I have enough information to analyze the UX aspects of this PR. Let me look at the critical UX issues:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7846772Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7848033Z   1. **Broken install** - The `.gitignore` file at `packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/extensions/issue-monitor/.gitignore` explicitly gitignores `dist/`. This is a blocking issue from a UX perspective - the install flow is fundamentally broken.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7849368Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7850017Z   2. **Two-command install flow** - The install requires `apm experimental enable canvas` first, then `apm install ... --trust-canvas-extensions`. 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7850626Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7851661Z   3. **`--trust-canvas-extensions` flag** - This grants permission to execute `gh` CLI commands on behalf of the user, which is a high-trust operation for approving PRs, merging, and posting comments. The README mentions it, but the flag name doesn't clearly convey what permissions it actually grants.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7852753Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7853609Z   4. **Hardcoded repository** - The extension hardcodes `REPO = "microsoft/apm"` in `extension.mjs`, yet the apm.yml description suggests it works with multiple GitHub repositories, and the README indicates it's currently scoped to a specific one.

2026-06-17T16:12:23.7912764Z [205] ◐ Now let me look at the CSRF token handling more carefully - specifically how it's embedded in the HTML and retrieved by the client. Let me check if there are any other potential security concerns.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7913959Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7924340Z   Let me look at the request handling for the `/api/issue/:n` path more carefully. The server reads the number from the URL regex match and passes it to ghExec. Is the number validated as a numeric only?
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7925199Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7925643Z   Looking at: `const issueMatch = req.url.match(/^\/api\/issue\/(\d+)$/);` - yes, `(\d+)` captures only digits.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7926121Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7926307Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7927799Z   The PR endpoint follows the same pattern with `(\d+)`, so that's also restricted to digits. But in the `/approve-workflow-runs` handler, the branch name comes from the client's JSON body and gets passed directly to the GitHub CLI command - even though `execFile` prevents shell injection, I should check if argument injection is possible through branch names like `--json` or `--limit`.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7929188Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7930643Z   With `execFile`, arguments are passed as array elements without shell interpretation, but a branch name starting with `--` could still be interpreted as a CLI flag by the `gh` command itself. So if someone passes `--json foo` as the branch, it would be treated as separate arguments rather than a branch name, which is a form of argument injection even without shell involvement.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7931983Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7934513Z   The same vulnerability exists in `/create-follow-up-issues` where `panelReview` data from the client body gets passed to `gh issue create` — if a title or body starts with `--`, it could inject unintended options. For the PR number validation in endpoints like `/approve-pr` and `/merge-when-ready`, the number gets JSON parsed and stringified, so `String({})` would produce `"[object Object]"`, but these invalid inputs would just fail the command rather than cause injection. The more critical issue is still the `branch` field in `/approve-workflow-runs`, where something like... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3989 chars]
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7937098Z

2026-06-17T16:12:23.7980217Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7981287Z [217] ◐ Let me also check the `/approve-workflow-runs` endpoint more carefully - it reads `branch` from the JSON body and passes it directly to `ghExec`. Since `execFile` is used, there's no shell injection, but argument injection is possible if `branch` starts with `--`. For example:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7982719Z   - `{ branch: "--help" }` would cause `gh run list --repo microsoft/apm --branch --help --limit 10 --json databaseId,conclusion` to fail (or print help)
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7983336Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7984142Z   Actually, `gh run list --branch --help` would most likely try to use `--help` as a branch name, not interpret it as a flag, because the argument parser already consumed `--branch` and expects the next value to be the branch name. 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7985043Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7986870Z   Though how the CLI actually parses this depends on the specific implementation - with modern CLIs like gh, `--help` might be treated as the value for `--branch`, or it could be interpreted as a flag depending on the parser. The real security picture here is that the CSRF token and origin check mean only the legitimate dashboard client can call these endpoints, and since the dashboard gets branch names from the actual gh CLI output, they'd be valid in normal operation. An attacker would need to already be on localhost to exploit this.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7988735Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7990118Z   Looking at `/approve-workflow-runs` specifically, the branch comes from the client's JSON body, which itself comes from GitHub API data that the server already provided - it's essentially the same value making a round trip. Injection would only be possible if GitHub itself had a malicious branch name or if there's client-side script injection modifying the value.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7991443Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7992020Z   The more pressing issue is prompt injection in `/refine-comment`, where user-supplied `draft` and `title` values get interpolated directly into the prompt.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7992809Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7993719Z   Now I'm examining the error handling to see if tokens might leak through error messages. The code catches errors and returns them as JSON strings, but I need to check whether the `gh` CLI might include authentication tokens in its error output when something fails.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7994698Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7994946Z   Looki... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2771 chars]
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7995185Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7995542Z [218] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && cat apm.yml | head -40; echo "---"; tail -20 apm.lock.yaml
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7995899Z

2026-06-17T16:12:23.7997078Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7998006Z [220] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also check the root README to see if there's any mention of the canvas/dashboard feature there (this PR adds it as a first-party package, so it might be worth checking if the root README should be updated).
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7998952Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7999152Z   Now let me form my findings:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7999395Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.7999774Z   **1. apm.yml description misrepresents scope (recommended)**
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8001631Z   The `apm.yml` description says "Live contributor dashboard canvas for GitHub repositories" -- generic, suggesting multi-repo applicability. But the code hardcodes `REPO = "microsoft/apm"` (extension.mjs line 19), the Navbar hardcodes `https://github.com/microsoft/apm`, and the README explicitly disclaims it as "scoped to microsoft/apm". The registry-facing artifact (apm.yml) therefore actively misleads potential adopters about reusability. 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8003235Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8004288Z   Someone discovering this through `apm search` would try installing it for their own repo and hit a wall. I'd recommend updating the description to be explicit: "Live contributor dashboard canvas for the microsoft/apm repository. Tracks issues, PRs, CI pipelines..." to set proper expectations upfront.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8005418Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8005666Z   **2. No screenshot or animated GIF in README (recommended)**
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8007252Z   The dashboard is a visual product (tabs, badges, comment composer). The README has zero visuals. For a canvas UI tool, the absence of a screenshot is the single biggest conversion blocker -- developers won't "Open the APM Contributor Dashboard" unless they know what they're opening. No screenshot = no trust. Adding a screenshot or animated GIF showing the dashboard in action would dramatically improve discoverability.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8008738Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8009018Z   **3. "Collaborative comment drafting with Copilot" buried (nit)**
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8010693Z   The strongest, most novel hook -- drafting comments with Copilot inline -- appears third in the feature list and nowhere in the lede. The README opens with "live contributor dashboard," which is useful but generic. The Copilot-native UX is what differentiates this from a plain GitHub web view. I'd lead with something like: "Ask Copilot to draft a review com... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5138 chars]

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8029754Z   This is a critical issue - the PR body claims "The package ships both a pre-built `dist/` (works immediately on `apm install`)" but the gitignore prevents dist from being committed. 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8030728Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8031130Z   However, let me re-read the README more carefully:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8031668Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8032698Z   > **Important:** always commit `dist/` alongside source changes. The dist is what end users get when they `apm install` -- if you update the source but not the dist, your changes will not reach users.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8033883Z    
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8036182Z   So there's a fundamental contradiction here - the `.gitignore` blocks `dist/` but the README explicitly instructs contributors to commit it. This creates a broken workflow where new contributors following normal git practices will never commit the dist, and when users run `apm install`, the extension won't actually work because the dist files are missing. This is a blocking issue that breaks the entire installation experience.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8038327Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8040278Z   Looking at the actual changed files in this PR, I don't see any dist/ directory committed at the path where it should be - `packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/extensions/issue-monitor/dist/`. The `.gitignore` at that location explicitly excludes dist, so the package being delivered is source-only with no runnable client, despite the claim that it works immediately on install. I need to examine the client permissions store and error handling to understand the full scope of the UX problems here.

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8055265Z   Looking at the error handling, I'm noticing some concerning patterns. In `extension.mjs`, the PR fetch operation catches all errors silently without any user feedback — the user could be viewing stale or missing PR data with no indication something went wrong. This is a significant UX gap that needs addressing.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8056416Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8057561Z   The session persistence functions also swallow errors completely. If `saveSessions` fails to write to disk, the user loses their session tracking without any warning. The `loadSessions` function handles failures more gracefully since it's expected to fail on first run, but the silent failures in persistence are problematic.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8058767Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8059008Z   Examining error handling in the workflow run fetch...
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8059324Z

That's the right approach. The only quirk is that `lastUpdated` doesn't get refreshed when an error occurs, so the timestamp stays stale alongside the error message, which is actually appropriate since nothing new was fetched.

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8118808Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8119265Z   Let me now also see what happens with the `DIST_DIR` in extension.mjs when dist/ doesn't exist. I saw that `extension.mjs` has:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8119930Z   ```js
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8120162Z   const DIST_DIR = join(__extensionDir, "dist");
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8120453Z   ```
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8120969Z   And `createHandler` takes `distDir: DIST_DIR`. And `serveStatic` does `readFileSync(filePath)` which would throw if the file doesn't exist.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8121546Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8121720Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8122403Z   So if dist/ doesn't exist, users will get 404s for all static assets and the index.html won't be served, leaving the canvas blank. This confirms the blocking issue.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8123045Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8123440Z   Now I have enough information to write a comprehensive review. Let me compile the final JSON response.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8123835Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8123956Z [234] ◆ ```json

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8138864Z [235] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive view of the PR. Let me analyze it from a Python Architect perspective. 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8139327Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8139508Z   Key observations:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8140276Z   1. This is NOT Python code - it's a JavaScript/Node.js canvas extension. The Python Architect panel is reviewing architectural patterns, not language-specific details.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8140951Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8142752Z   2. **Module-level mutable state in extension.mjs**: The `let issueData = []`, `let prData = []`, `let lastError = null`, `let lastUpdated = null`, `let pollTimer = null`, `let openInstanceCount = 0` are all module-level mutable state. This is equivalent to global variables in Python - they're shared state that multiple components (HTTP handler, polling timer, canvas actions) all read and write. The `server-handler.mjs` uses dependency injection correctly, but `extension.mjs` acts as an unencapsulated service locator/global state container.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8144616Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8144790Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8145945Z   3. **Hardcoded repository constraint**: The repo is hardcoded to "microsoft/apm", which severely limits the package's utility despite its description claiming it works for any GitHub repository. This creates a fundamental mismatch between the package's stated purpose and its actual implementation.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8147056Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8147899Z   4. **Build artifacts in version control**: The dist/ directory is checked in alongside source code, which obscures diffs, introduces unverifiable content, and creates a disconnect between what's actually running and what the source code shows.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8148823Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8149404Z   5. **Security consideration**: The server only listens on localhost (127.0.0.1), which is appropriate for a local canvas extension, but this constraint is worth noting.

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8157947Z   <Show when={prs().length > 0} fallback={<div class="empty">Loading pull requests...</div>}>
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8158368Z   ```
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8158543Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8159820Z   This is a significant UX issue — when `prResource()` is in error state or returns an empty array (because `fetchAndMatchPRs` silently failed), the user sees "Loading pull requests..." indefinitely, with no timeout, no error message, and no way to distinguish "loading" from "failed to load".
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8161042Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8161572Z   Compare with `IssuesTab.jsx` which doesn't have this specific fallback shown, but the `App.jsx` shows an error bar from `issueResource()?.error`.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8162178Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8162347Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8163698Z   The PR tab displays "Loading pull requests..." even after the fetch has permanently failed, creating a misleading empty state. Meanwhile, the issues tab defaults to an empty array when `issueResource()` errors, but the error itself gets surfaced in the app's error bar. The key issue is that `fetchAndMatchPRs` catches all failures without updating `lastError`, so users never see what went wrong.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8165104Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8166155Z   I should also note that `PrsTab.jsx` uses a generic "Loading..." message as its zero-items fallback, which persists indefinitely when the PR fetch silently fails—there's no timeout or way to distinguish between still-loading and actually-failed states.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8167117Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8169986Z   Then there's `App.jsx`, which never renders the error state from `prResource`. In Solid.js, I need to access the error via `prResource.error` directly, not `prResource()?.error`. Let me look at how the resource is actually imported and used to get this right. `/api/issues` would expose the Solid.js resource error, but the app doesn't check for that either. On the PR side, `fetchAndMatchPRs` silently catches all errors without updating `lastError`, so the response always shows `error: null` regardless of whether PR data is missing or stale — and the UI doesn't check the Solid.js resource error for PRs either. Both gaps stem from errors not being propagated through any layer.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8172315Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8172777Z   The key issues are: `PrsTab.jsx` shows only "Loading pull requests..." wit... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3162 chars]

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8193326Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8193496Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8195846Z   I'm seeing some potential security issues here. There's prompt injection risk in the `/refine-comment` and `/start-session` endpoints where user-controlled data like GitHub issue titles and draft content get interpolated directly into prompts without any sanitization. That could let someone craft a malicious issue title to manipulate the Copilot session. There's also an argument injection concern with the `branch` parameter in `/approve-workflow-runs` — while execFile prevents shell injection, if the branch value starts with `--`, it could be parsed as a flag instead of a value.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8197911Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8199501Z   The gh CLI's stderr output is being sent back to clients as-is in error responses, which could potentially leak sensitive information, though typically not actual tokens. And there's a correctness issue where the dist/ folder is now gitignored, so the package won't include pre-built client code on install despite the README claiming it works immediately — users would need to build it themselves.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8201060Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8202574Z   Beyond that, there's an unversioned external dependency on the Copilot SDK extension that isn't locked in any APM-controlled lockfile, so there's no way to verify its integrity or audit the supply chain. If that package gets compromised, the extension would execute attacker code. The new `--trust-canvas-extensions` flag introduces a trust model that lets the extension run gh CLI commands on the us... 2026-06-17T16:12:23.8209287Z   "**No arbitrary code execution.** APM does not execute scripts from packages, evaluate expressions in templates, or run downloaded code. (**Canvas exception:** the experimental `canvas` primitive deploys executable `extension.mjs` (Node.js) code to `.github/extensions/` or `~/.copilot/extensions/`; this surface is gated by both the `canvas` experimental flag and `--trust-canvas-extensions` for dependency-provided canvases. 2026-06-17T16:12:23.8222516Z [246] ◐ Let me also check if the `gh` error output from failed commands might contain tokens. The `ghExec` function rejects with `new Error(stderr || err.message)`. `gh` CLI is quite careful about not putting tokens in error messages. Let me check if there's a concern here. Actually since gh CLI uses tokens stored in a keyring/config (not environment variables in normal flow), and error messages from gh are just API error responses, this should be relatively safe. But it's still not ideal to pass raw CLI stderr to clients.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8224316Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8224776Z   Let me now also check for one more concern: the `/run-panel` endpoint which calls `gh pr edit ... --add-label panel-review`. 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8225313Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8227211Z   Looking at how the `number` parameter gets passed through—when it's converted to a string and passed to execFile, it becomes a single argument regardless of its content. So even if someone tries to inject something like `"123 --repo other/repo"`, it would just fail because gh would interpret that entire string as the PR number. The real risk would be if the parameter had a custom `toString()` method that could manipulate the command, but that's not a practical injection vector here.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8228891Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8230157Z   The bigger issue is more about validation—if `number` is something like `3.14`, converting it to a string would pass `"3.14"` to gh, which would fail validation anyway. Adding explicit integer validation would be more robust, though this is more of a functional concern than a security one.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8231211Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8233272Z   I'm also noticing the `decodeURIComponent` call in the static file server could throw if the URL has malformed percent encoding.  This exception isn't caught, so it would crash the request handler and potentially the entire extension process. Since the server only listens on localhost with CSRF protection in place, the attack surface is limited, but it's still a potential DoS vector. crash because Node.js HTTP server wraps request handlers, but in Node.js >= 15, an unhandled rejection from an async handler causes process exit by default. So this is actually a cras... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4211 chars]

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8239158Z [249] ◐ Now I have a clear picture. The apm.lock.yaml changes only add `declared_license: MIT` to 5 existing packages and bumps the apm_version from 0.19.0 to 0.20.0. 2026-06-17T16:12:23.8240623Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8242507Z   This is actually a **blocking** finding from a supply chain perspective: the new package ships without being tracked in the apm.lock.yaml. APM's security model requires that all deployed files have content-hash provenance via the lockfile. If the dashboard package is installed by users via `apm install microsoft/apm/packages/apm-contributor-dashboard`, the install will happen but won't be recorded in the project's apm.lock.yaml. The lock.yaml is the "source of truth for resolved identity" per APM's security model.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8244331Z    
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8245845Z   Actually, I'm reconsidering this. The microsoft/apm repo's apm.lock.yaml doesn't need to track the dashboard package itself - it tracks what microsoft/apm installs as dependencies. Since the dashboard package is part of microsoft/apm, users installing it into their own projects would get their own lock entries in their repo's apm.lock.yaml. The microsoft/apm project only needs to list its own dependencies, not the packages it provides.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8247433Z

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8251522Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8252197Z [251] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && cat packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/extensions/issue-monitor/server-handler.mjs | grep -n "decodeURIComponent" -A 5 | head -20
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8252855Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8253272Z [252] ◐ Now I have the complete picture of the server-handler.mjs. Let me analyze the auth-relevant details carefully.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8253783Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8253979Z   **Critical findings to analyze:**
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8254240Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8254437Z   ### 1. CSRF Protection Model
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8254744Z   The CSRF protection is implemented at lines 81-97:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8255170Z   - Checks if the request method is POST and the URL is in WRITE_ENDPOINTS
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8255644Z   - Checks the Origin header against a whitelist (localhost/127.0.0.1)
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8256079Z   - Validates the CSRF token in the `x-canvas-token` header
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8256399Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8256582Z   **Issues:**
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8258209Z   - The Origin check is conditional: `if (origin && ...)` - if no Origin header is present, the check is SKIPPED. This is a significant security issue. In browsers, cross-site requests would typically set the Origin header, but requests from non-browser clients (curl, custom scripts, etc.) might not set it. Since CSRF tokens are also validated, this is somewhat mitigated, but...
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8260848Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8261185Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8262825Z   - Actually, looking at the logic more carefully: the origin check only rejects if an origin header is present AND it doesn't match the whitelist. If there's no origin header at all, that check passes through. Then the CSRF token validation happens separately and is always enforced, so even if the origin check is bypassed, the token check still needs to pass.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8264135Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8265595Z   The CSRF token is embedded in the HTML as `window.__CANVAS_TOKEN__`, which is only accessible from the same origin in a browser context. For an attacker to exploit this, they'd need to either extract the token from the HTML or somehow discover the random port the server is listening on. Since the port is only known to the canvas extension and the served canvas, an attacker on localhost would face a significant barrier in discovering it.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8267130Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8268744Z   Looking at potential bypass vectors: a `null` origin from a file:// page would be blocked correctly, an empty origin would still require a valid CSRF token, and something like `http://localhost.evil.com` wouldn't match the regex pattern that specifically checks for `127.0.0.1` or `localhost`. The token is embedded as a script in the HTML via string replacement, which work... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 27456 chars]
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8270345Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8271530Z [253] ✓ $ python3 << 'EOF' import json with open('/tmp/1781711436227-copilot-tool-output-a2b8b7.txt') as f: data = json.load(f) content = data[0]['content'][1]['resource']['text'] lines = content.split('\n') # Focus on key sections: CSRF (79-97), permissions (373-386), approve-workflow-runs (301-318), readBod...
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8272560Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8273086Z [254] ✓ github-get_file_contents(owner: microsoft, path: packages/apm-contributor-dashboard/.apm/..., ref: refs/pull/1825/head, repo: apm)
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8273617Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8273981Z [255] ◐ Now I have all the information I need. Let me finalize my analysis and produce the JSON response.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8274441Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8274632Z   Key observations:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8276125Z   1. **CSRF guard on line 81** uses `WRITE_ENDPOINTS.has(req.url)` where `req.url` can include query params (e.g., `/merge-when-ready?x=1`). This would bypass the CSRF check, but the endpoint handler at line 322 also uses `req.url === "/merge-when-ready"` which wouldn't match either. So it's inert today but fragile. Note that the static file serving at line 432 correctly strips query params with `.split("?")[0]`, but the CSRF check doesn't.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8277620Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8277799Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8278471Z   2. **Origin check on line 85** is `if (origin && ...)` - requests without an origin header skip the origin validation entirely, leaving only the CSRF token as protection for non-browser clients.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8279723Z   3. **Permissions endpoint at line 375** caches results indefinitely with no expiration, so revoked permissions remain active until the extension restarts.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8280766Z   4. **`/approve-workflow-runs` at line 305** takes an arbitrary `branch` parameter from the request body without validating it matches the PR being approved.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8281697Z   5. **Write endpoints skip server-side permission re-validation** and rely entirely on GitHub to enforce access controls.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8282571Z   6. The token injection at line 437 is safe since it's hex-only with no XSS risk, and the string replacement correctly targets the closing head tag.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8283291Z   7. Using `execFile` throughout prevents shell injection vulnerabilities.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8284025Z   8. The hardcoded `"microsoft/apm"` repository in extension.mjs can't be overridden by users.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8284763Z   9. Path traversal protection correctly normalizes paths and validates them against the distribution directory.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8285509Z   10. The readBody function lacks server-side size limits, though this is a minor concern for a localhost tool.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8286039Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8286573Z   Now I'm compiling the final security summary. Counting characters to verify the summary fits... Now I'm putting together the final JSON output.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8287094Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8287401Z [256] ◆ Now I have a complete picture of the auth surface. Let me compile my findings.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8287728Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8288854Z [257] ✓ $ python3 << 'EOF' # Verify: does POST /merge-when-ready?x=1 bypass CSRF and reach the handler? # Line 81: WRITE_ENDPOINTS.has("/merge-when-ready?x=1") -> false -> CSRF skipped # Line 322: req.url === "/merge-when-ready" -> false (mismatches) -> handler not reached # Falls through to static serving. S...
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8290021Z 
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8290494Z [258] ◐ Now let me check whether `decodeURIComponent` is actually wrapped in any outer try-catch. Looking at the code structure:
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8291044Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8291297Z   1. The `handler` is an `async function handler(req, res)`
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8291949Z   2. The `decodeURIComponent(req.url.split("?")[0])` call is at line 432, which is outside any try-catch
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8292487Z   3. If it throws a URIError, the async handler would reject
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8292798Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8293865Z   In Node.js HTTP server, when an async request handler throws, what happens? In Node.js, `http.createServer(requestListener)` - if requestListener is an async function and the promise rejects, Node.js doesn't automatically handle it. The rejection would be unhandled, which in Node.js >= 15 exits the process.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8294989Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8295165Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8296993Z   So technically this is a DoS vulnerability, though it's limited to localhost and requires someone to deliberately send a malformed request with invalid percent-encoding. A browser making a GET request to something like `http://127.0.0.1:<port>/%` would trigger it, since GET requests aren't protected by CSRF checks the way POST requests are. The attacker would need to brute-force the port number across all 65,535 possibilities, which makes this more of a theoretical vulnerability than a practical exploit. But it's still worth flagging.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8298865Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8300121Z   Now let me trace through the prompt injection issue more carefully. The `/start-session` endpoint constructs a prompt by directly interpolating the `number` and `title` fields from the request body into the message sent to the session, which means an attacker could inject arbitrary instructions through those fields.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8301307Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8302809Z   The attack chain requires creating a GitHub issue with a malicious title on the target repository, then getting the user to click "Start Session" on that issue in the dashboard. Once the crafted title reaches the Copilot agent's prompt, it could potentially manipulate the agent's behavior. This is a genuine prompt injection vulnerability, though the severity depends on how exploitable it is in practice given the prerequisites involved.
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8304865Z   
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8305239Z   Now I'm checking the CSRF token injection in... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5813 chars]

The doc writer catches a critical blocker where the README claims pre-built dist/ files ship but they're actually gitignored, plus the dev workflow test command is incomplete.

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8416338Z   {
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8417191Z     "headline": "Adds APM's first first-party canvas package -- a Copilot contributor dashboard with 186 JS tests and a sound security core -- but ships non-functional on apm install because dist/ is gitignored; rework needed on three fronts.",
2026-06-17T16:12:23.8424140Z     "arbitration": "This PR ships APM's most significant first-party proof-point to date: a Copilot-native contributor dashboard that demonstrates the canvas extension model in production, using APM to manage APM's own open-source contributor workflow. The 186-test suite, CSRF guard, execFile boundary, and path-traversal validation represent a genuinely strong security and quality baseline. Two panelists (devx-ux-expert, doc-writer) independently reached the same hard stop: dist/ is gitignored and not committed, meaning every user who follows the README install instructions receives a canvas extension that mounts with no client assets and returns 404 on every page request. The README simultaneously claims pre-built assets ship with the package. This is a correctness regression for every installer on the current commit.\n\nPanel convergence is high on three required fixes. First, the dist/ absence is the highest-priority mechanical issue: either commit a built dist/ at merge time or add a build step to the extension activation hook, and update the README to accurately describe the build requirement. Second, three panelists (supply-chain-security-expert, python-architect, auth-expert) independently flagged that user-controlled GitHub issue/PR title and user draft are interpolated raw into session.send() prompts in /refine-comment; the auth-expert classified this as self-injection-only (nit) but the supply-chain framing is stronger -- GitHub issue titles are adversary-controlled inputs in any multi-contributor repo and must be treated as untrusted before they reach a Copilot session. Third, apm.yml and the README both claim the package works for any GitHub repository while REPO is hardcoded to microsoft/apm; three panelist... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 8930 chars]

2026-06-17T16:12:23.8449807Z   **Summary:** `needs_rework` — two blocking-severity findings from devx-ux-expert and doc-writer converge on the same root cause: `dist/` is gitignored and not committed, so `apm install` delivers a non-functional canvas extension (every page request returns 404). The README simultaneously claims pre-built assets ship. Three additional high-signal recommended fixes: prompt injection via GitHub-controlled issue titles into `session.send()`, hardcoded `REPO = "microsoft/apm"` vs. the apm.yml generic-scope claim, and a fragile CSRF query-string bypass. The underlying contribution (186 JS tests, sound security core, strong dogfooding story) is worth shipping quickly once those three root issues are addressed.
